verse_text: And on the seventh day God finished the works He made, and He rested on the seventh day from all the works He made.

links.context: When the Lord commanded the Hebrews, in the fourth of the Ten Commandments, to 'Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy,' He also gave them the reason: 'For in six days the Lord made heaven and earth, the sea, and everything in them, and rested on the seventh day. Therefore, the Lord blessed the Sabbath day and hallowed it' ([[EXO.20:8]], [[EXO.20:11]]; cf. [[GEN.2:1]]-3). When Moses restated the Ten Commandments in Deuteronomy 5, he added another reason: 'Remember, you were a servant in the land of Egypt, and the Lord your God brought you out from there by a mighty hand and by an outstretched arm; therefore, the Lord your God ordered you to guard the Sabbath day and to sanctify it' (5:15). The Hebrews were called to 'remember' ([[EXO.20:8]]), to 'keep' ([[LEV.19:3]], 30), and to 'hallow' or's anctify' ([[JER.17:19]]-27; [[EZK.20:19]], [[EZK.20:20]]; [[NEH.13:15]]-22) the Sabbath by resting from almost every kind of work. God provided them this sacred time each week to help them contemplate His awesome work in creation and their miraculous deliverance from Egypt. Stipulating the faithful observance of the Sabbath was one of the main ways God ordained to reinforce the people's covenant with Him ([[EXO.31:12]]-17; cf. [[LEV.24:8]]). Originally, communal worship was not linked with Sabbath observance; but with the development of the synagogue, probably during the Hebrews' exile in Babylon (sixth century BC), the Sabbath naturally became the day for synagogue worship, as it is for the Jews today. #### Sunday, The Day Of Worship

This reference is to the rebellion of those who left Egypt during the Exodus. Due to their unbelief, the whole generation (v. 10) was forbidden to enter into Canaan, the promised land, and thus could not enter God's rest (v. 11). Rest (Gr. sabbatismos) literally means a Sabbath rest or Sabbath observance. There are three types of God's rest known to the Jews: (1) the Sabbath rest, the day on which God rested from His works ([[GEN.2:2]], [[GEN.2:3]]); (2) the rest from Egyptian bondage, which the Israelites coming out of Egypt experienced in Canaan; (3) the rest in the kingdom, the ultimate Sabbath rest in heaven established by Messiah. Hebrews uses this OT quote (Ps 94:7-11) concerning Canaan to refer to the rest in the Kingdom of heaven. Significantly, we experience this rest now as we ascend to God in worship (4:4-11).

The first day of the week is Sunday, or the Lord's Day. The remembrance of this day in Christian tradition does not replace the Sabbath, but completes it. Saturday still remains the Sabbath, the blessed day on which Christ rested in the tomb, just as He rested when He completed the creation of the universe ([[GEN.2:2]]). Because of the Resurrection, Sunday is also known in the Church as the eighth day, which symbolizes eternity, a day without end.

His rest in the tomb fulfills the image of His birth in a cave (see 2:7) and reveals the ultimate purpose of His coming. The faith of the women, while stronger than that of the hiding disciples, was still imperfect in that they prepared for the corruption of Christ's body. Sabbath: As God rested from His work of creation on the original Sabbath ([[GEN.2:1]]-3), so now Christ rests from the work of the new creation on the Sabbath. Thus He gives the Sabbath its ultimate meaning and fulfills the Law even in death. At Matins of Holy Saturday we sing, "This is the most blessed Sabbath on which Christ sleeps in order to rise on the third day."
